claims.claim_text: The December 2020 airline scaling incident with 3x traffic, 8-minute bootstraps, and $2.3 million in missed revenue is presented as a real event.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In December 2020, a major US airline was running its booking system on a fleet of EC2 instances managed by a custom deployment pipeline. During a holiday travel surge, traffic spiked 3x beyond projections. The operations team scrambled to launch new instances, but the bootstrapping process -- installing dependencies, pulling container images, registering with the load balancer -- took 8 minutes per instance. For 8 minutes, customers saw timeout errors. Bookings were lost. The post-incident review estimated $2.3 million in missed revenue during that single scaling event.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams that rely on slow instance bootstrapping can struggle during sudden traffic spikes, because installing dependencies, pulling images, and registering with the load balancer can take long enough to cause customer-facing errors and lost business.
claims.rationale: This is a detailed named-event-style anecdote with precise timing and revenue figures but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The same airline allegedly migrated to ECS on Fargate six weeks later and then handled a 4x holiday surge with new containers launching in under 15 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: They migrated to ECS on Fargate six weeks later. The next holiday surge came, and Fargate launched new containers in under 15 seconds. No EC2 instances to bootstrap. No AMIs to maintain. No capacity planning for unpredictable peaks. The traffic spike was 4x that year. Nobody noticed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Moving container workloads to ECS on Fargate can remove instance bootstrapping work and make it easier to absorb sudden traffic spikes without managing AMIs or scaling hosts yourself.
claims.rationale: This continues the unsupported anecdote with precise timeline and performance claims that are not traceable to a real source.

claims.claim_text: Task definitions are versioned and each update creates a new revision.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Architecture Overview, Core Concepts, Task Definition
claims.rationale: AWS documents task definition families and sequential revisions directly.

claims.claim_text: A task can contain multiple containers that share the same network namespace and communicate via localhost.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Architecture Overview, Core Concepts, Task
claims.anchor_text: One task can contain multiple containers that share the same network namespace and can communicate via `localhost`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In `awsvpc` mode, a task can contain multiple containers that share the task network namespace and can communicate via `localhost`.
claims.rationale: The blanket statement overgeneralizes behavior that AWS documents specifically for `awsvpc` task networking.

claims.claim_text: An ECS service maintains a desired task count, replaces failed tasks, and can integrate with a load balancer.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Architecture Overview, Core Concepts, Service
claims.rationale: The ECS services guide explicitly describes desired count maintenance, task replacement, and optional load balancers.

claims.claim_text: Fargate pricing is per-second with a one-minute minimum and is based on CPU and memory resources.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Pricing row
claims.rationale: The Fargate FAQ states that billing is per second with a one-minute minimum and based on provisioned resources.

claims.claim_text: Fargate cold starts take 45-90 seconds because of microVM startup, ENI provisioning, and image pulls.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Startup time row
claims.anchor_text: 45-90 seconds (cold start: microVM + ENI + image pull)
claims.suggested_rewrite: typically tens of seconds on a cold start, depending on networking and image pull time
claims.rationale: AWS documents the factors that affect startup time, but this exact timing range is not stably documented as an official benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Fargate does not support GPUs, and SageMaker should be used for GPU workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, GPU support row
claims.anchor_text: No (as of 2026, use SageMaker for GPU)
claims.suggested_rewrite: No (use the EC2 launch type for GPU workloads).
claims.rationale: AWS documents that Fargate tasks do not support the `gpu` parameter, but the SageMaker prescription is extra guidance not directly backed here.

claims.claim_text: Fargate task size tops out at 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Max task size row
claims.rationale: AWS documents the valid Fargate CPU and memory ranges through 16 vCPU and 120 GB.

claims.claim_text: Fargate supports only specific CPU and memory combinations rather than arbitrary pairings.
claims.span_hint: section: CPU and Memory Combinations for Fargate table
claims.rationale: AWS publishes the allowed Fargate task-level CPU and memory combinations in the task definition reference.

claims.claim_text: The task execution role is used by ECS or Fargate agents to pull images, fetch referenced secrets, and send logs.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Roles: Execution Role vs Task Role, execution role diagram and commands
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents these execution-role use cases.

claims.claim_text: The task role is what application code inside the container uses to call AWS APIs.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Roles: Execution Role vs Task Role, task role diagram and commands
claims.rationale: AWS documents the task IAM role as the role made available to containers for application API calls.

claims.claim_text: Fargate tasks in private subnets need outbound connectivity, such as NAT or equivalent private connectivity, to pull images and reach external services.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, explanation of `assignPublicIp: DISABLED`
claims.rationale: AWS documents that Fargate tasks must have a route for outbound access when they need to pull images.

claims.claim_text: The production pattern is to never expose tasks directly to the internet.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, explanation of `assignPublicIp: DISABLED`
claims.anchor_text: This is the production pattern -- never expose tasks directly to the internet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For internet-facing services, expose the load balancer rather than the tasks themselves.
claims.rationale: This is prescriptive security guidance stated as an absolute rather than a sourced AWS rule.

claims.claim_text: Using `maximumPercent: 200` and `minimumHealthyPercent: 100` means zero-downtime rolling deployments.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, explanation of deployment configuration
claims.suggested_rewrite: This configuration is designed to support rolling deployments without reducing healthy capacity, assuming the new tasks become healthy in time.
claims.rationale: AWS documents how these percentages control replacement behavior, but absolute zero downtime depends on workload health and capacity.

claims.claim_text: Deployment circuit breaker was added in 2021 and should be enabled on every service.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, explanation of `deploymentCircuitBreaker`
claims.anchor_text: This was added in 2021 and should be enabled on every service.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This feature became generally available in late 2020 and is a strong default for many services.
claims.rationale: The date is wrong and the universal recommendation is opinionated rather than a directly documented rule.

claims.claim_text: ECS integrates with AWS Cloud Map for DNS-based service discovery so services can find each other by name instead of hardcoded IPs.
claims.span_hint: section: Service Discovery, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: The ECS service discovery docs describe Cloud Map integration and DNS naming directly.

claims.claim_text: ECS Exec uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager under the hood.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Exec: Debugging Running Containers, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS documents the ECS Exec architecture in terms of Systems Manager Session Manager.

claims.claim_text: Blue/green with CodeDeploy gives you the ability to instantly roll back.
claims.span_hint: section: Deployment Strategies, Blue/Green with CodeDeploy heading
claims.anchor_text: For production services where you want the ability to instantly roll back:
claims.suggested_rewrite: For production services where you want controlled traffic shifting and a fast rollback path:
claims.rationale: AWS documents verification and traffic shifting, but 'instantly' is stronger than the documented behavior.

claims.claim_text: Fargate Spot uses spare AWS capacity, can be up to 70% cheaper, and can interrupt tasks with a two-minute warning.
claims.span_hint: section: Fargate Spot: Saving Up to 70%
claims.rationale: AWS's launch material states the discount, spare-capacity model, and two-minute interruption notice.

claims.claim_text: With `base: 2` on FARGATE and weights `1:3`, the example places two baseline tasks on Fargate and distributes the remaining tasks roughly 1:3 across Fargate and Fargate Spot.
claims.span_hint: section: Fargate Spot, explanation below capacity provider strategy example
claims.rationale: AWS documents that base is satisfied first and remaining tasks are distributed according to weight ratios.

claims.claim_text: ECS predates Kubernetes' public release, but the sentence gives the chronology incorrectly.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1, first sentence block
claims.anchor_text: **ECS predates Kubernetes' public release.** Amazon launched ECS in April 2015, just months after Kubernetes 1.0 was released in July 2015.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **ECS reached general availability before Kubernetes 1.0.** Amazon ECS became generally available in April 2015, and Kubernetes 1.0 shipped in July 2015.
claims.rationale: The original sentence is internally inconsistent on dates, so it should be corrected rather than cited as written.

claims.claim_text: ECS runs more containers on AWS than EKS, many of the largest AWS customers including Amazon.com use ECS internally, and Amazon retail processes millions of transactions on ECS.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1, remaining sentences
claims.anchor_text: While Kubernetes won the industry mindshare war, ECS runs more containers on AWS than EKS does. Many of the largest AWS customers -- including Amazon.com itself -- use ECS internally rather than Kubernetes. AWS's own retail platform processes millions of transactions using ECS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: ECS remains a widely used AWS container platform, including for large-scale internal Amazon workloads that AWS has publicly discussed.
claims.rationale: These are specific market-share and internal-usage assertions without a directly matching authoritative source on the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Fargate tasks execute on Firecracker microVMs, the same virtualization technology family used by Lambda.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2, sentence 2
claims.rationale: AWS states in the Firecracker launch material that Lambda uses Firecracker and Fargate tasks execute on Firecracker microVMs.

claims.claim_text: Firecracker can launch a microVM in under 125 milliseconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2, sentence 3
claims.rationale: AWS's Firecracker launch blog gives the under-125 ms figure directly.

claims.claim_text: In `awsvpc` mode, each task gets its own ENI with a private IP address and can have security groups applied at task granularity.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3, first two sentences
claims.rationale: AWS documents the per-task ENI, private IP assignment, and task-level security-group behavior for `awsvpc`.

claims.claim_text: Before `awsvpc`, `bridge` and `host` modes did not let you apply security groups to individual tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3, final sentence
claims.rationale: AWS states that `awsvpc` is the mode you use to assign security groups to tasks.

claims.claim_text: ECS Exec was one of the most requested ECS features, with hundreds of GitHub reactions over three years before release in March 2021.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4, first sentence
claims.anchor_text: **ECS Exec was one of the most requested features in ECS history**, with the GitHub issue gathering hundreds of thumbs-up reactions over three years before it was finally released in March 2021.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **ECS Exec was released in March 2021**, giving ECS users a built-in way to run commands in running containers.
claims.rationale: The popularity ranking and reaction-history framing are not reliably backed by a single authoritative source, but the release date is.

claims.claim_text: ECS Exec launched in March 2021 and lets users run commands directly in running containers without interacting with the host, opening inbound ports, or managing SSH keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4, context sentence
claims.rationale: AWS's launch announcement states the release date and the security model for ECS Exec.

claims.claim_text: ECS Exec works by starting SSM agent components from the ECS or Fargate side, so you do not need to add an SSH server or extra agent binaries to your image.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4, final sentence
claims.rationale: The ECS Exec architecture docs explain that the required SSM agent binaries are bind-mounted and started by ECS or Fargate.

claims.claim_text: Without deployment circuit breaker, a bad deployment can keep retrying failing task launches indefinitely.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Not enabling deployment circuit breaker'
claims.rationale: AWS's circuit-breaker announcement describes the prior behavior as repeated retries that could lead to indefinite deployment delays.

claims.claim_text: Task-definition health checks and ALB health checks serve different roles, and ECS can wait on both before counting a task as healthy.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'No health check in task definition'
claims.rationale: AWS documents how container health checks and load-balancer health checks are both considered for services using a load balancer.

claims.claim_text: Without `linuxParameters.initProcessEnabled`, containers may mishandle SIGTERM during deployments and should always enable it.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Not setting `linuxParameters.initProcessEnabled`'
claims.anchor_text: Without an init process (PID 1 signal handling), your container may not handle SIGTERM gracefully during deployments, leading to dropped connections. Always enable it
claims.suggested_rewrite: Running an init process helps forward signals and reap child processes, which can improve shutdown behavior during deployments.
claims.rationale: AWS documents signal forwarding and process reaping, but the universal 'Always enable it' recommendation is stronger than the sourced behavior.

claims.claim_text: Scale-out cooldown should be 30-60 seconds and scale-in cooldown 300-600 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Setting scale-in cooldown too low'
claims.anchor_text: Set scale-out cooldown to 30-60s and scale-in cooldown to 300-600s
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use shorter scale-out cooldowns than scale-in cooldowns, then tune the exact values to your traffic patterns.
claims.rationale: AWS explains the different intent of scale-out and scale-in cooldowns, but these exact numeric bands are tuning guidance rather than official defaults.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECS FAQs
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives the official high-level model for ECS, Fargate, service discovery, and observability.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECS Task Networking Options for Fargate
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers ENIs, public versus private subnet behavior, and the networking assumptions behind Fargate services.

further_reading.title: Monitor Amazon ECS Containers with ECS Exec
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how ECS Exec works, what IAM it needs, and its operational limitations.

notes: The module contains two unsupported incident-style anecdotes, a few overstated operational absolutes, and one incorrect chronology sentence in the 'Did You Know' section. Several conceptual claims were narrowed to match AWS's documented scope, especially around `awsvpc` networking and deployment behavior.
